statement.p: Currently, an artist can sell 280 paintings every year at the price of {\$70.00} per painting. Each time he raises the price per painting by {\$10.00}, he sells 10 fewer paintings every year.

Assume he will raise the price per painting x times, then he will sell 280-10x paintings every year at the price of 70+10x dollars. His yearly income can be modeled by the equation: i=(70+10x)(280-10x) where i stands for his yearly income in dollars. If the artist wants to earn {\$21{,}600.00} per year from selling paintings, what new price should he set?
To earn {\$21{,}600.00} per year, the artist could sell his paintings at two different prices. The lower price is per painting, and the higher price is per painting.

solution.p: If the artist wants to make {\$21{,}600.00} per year, the equation becomes:
We will change the equation to standard form:
Next, we can solve this quadratic equation with the Quadratic Formula. Identify that a=-100,b=2100,c=-2000.
If the artist raises the price 1 times, the new price would be 70+10\cdot1=80 dollars per painting.
If the artist raises the price 20 times, the new price would be 70+10\cdot20=270 dollars per painting.
To earn {\$21{,}600.00} per year, the artist could sell his paintings at two different prices. The lower price is {\$80.00} per painting, and the higher price is {\$270.00} dollars per painting.
